topic: Area of Study 1: Western Classical Music 1600-1910
subject: music

items.q: Does 'Classical music' mean all old orchestral music?
items.correct: Strictly, the Classical era is the specific period of roughly 1750-1820; Baroque and Romantic are different styles with different features.
items.misconception: Classical is one big style covering everything from Bach to Tchaikovsky.
items.random_wrong: Classical music is any music played on vinyl records.
items.why: Exam answers must place works in the correct era by their features; lumping 1600-1910 together prevents accurate comparisons of style.

items.q: Is a harpsichord just an early, quieter piano?
items.correct: No - the harpsichord plucks its strings, so touch cannot vary the dynamics, unlike the piano's hammers.
items.misconception: Harpsichord and piano work the same way, one is simply older.
items.random_wrong: The harpsichord is a brass instrument played with valves.
items.why: The plucking mechanism explains the terraced dynamics of Baroque keyboard music and why the piano's touch-sensitive hammers were a revolution.

items.q: Is Romantic music all about romantic love?
items.correct: No - 'Romantic' refers to the artistic movement prizing emotion, imagination, nature and the supernatural, expressed in expanded musical means.
items.misconception: Romantic-era pieces are all love songs.
items.random_wrong: Romantic music was only performed at weddings.
items.why: The era's name comes from Romanticism in art and literature; its subjects range from heroism to landscape, so defining it by love songs misreads the period.

items.q: Which instruments typically played the basso continuo?
items.why: Correct answer: Harpsichord with cello. “Trumpet with timpani” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Programme music is best described as what?
items.correct: Instrumental music depicting a story or scene
items.misconception: Music printed in the concert programme
items.why: Correct answer: Instrumental music depicting a story or scene. “Music printed in the concert programme” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Vivaldi's 'The Four Seasons' belongs to which era?
items.why: Correct answer: Baroque. “Romantic” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What are the three main sections of sonata form?
items.why: Correct answer: Exposition, development, recapitulation. “Verse, chorus, bridge” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many violin concertos make up Vivaldi's 'The Four Seasons'?
items.why: The real figure is 4 concertos. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
